title_en: Worship Law: Schools in Dialogue on Prayer and Fasting
title_ar: فقه العبادات: المذاهب في حوار حول الصلاة والصوم
content_en:
The worship law sections of Al-Istidhkar are among its most illuminating, presenting the positions of the major legal schools on the contested questions of purification, prayer, fasting, and pilgrimage in systematic dialogue with one another. The purification chapters begin with the positions of all major schools on what substances and states require ritual cleanliness, how purification is achieved, and what conditions modify these requirements. Ibn Abd al-Barr's presentation of these positions alongside their textual bases allows readers to understand both what the schools teach and why they teach it.

The prayer sections of Al-Istidhkar address the many contested questions of prayer methodology with exceptional thoroughness. The positions of the Maliki, Hanafi, Shafi'i, and Hanbali schools on the raising of hands, the placement of hands during the standing position, the audible and silent recitations, the conduct of the Friday prayer, and dozens of other disputed points are all presented with their supporting evidence and then assessed. Ibn Abd al-Barr's willingness to note where the evidence clearly favors one school's position over another, even when that school is not his own, gives these sections a credibility that more partisan commentaries cannot achieve.

The fasting law sections of Al-Istidhkar cover the conditions of the obligatory Ramadan fast and the voluntary fasts in comparative perspective, noting where the schools agree and where they diverge. The treatment of the conditions that break the fast, the permissibility of various acts during fasting hours, and the rulings governing the expiatory acts required when the fast is broken are all addressed with attention to the positions of all major schools and the hadith evidence supporting each.

The pilgrimage sections are extensive and address the many complex questions of ritual performance that divided early juristic opinion. Ibn Abd al-Barr's comparative presentation of how each school understands the obligations and recommended acts of hajj, and what evidence each school adduces for its positions, is one of the most useful resources available for understanding the diversity of early Islamic pilgrimage jurisprudence.
